name: Užgavėnės
city: Nationwide (major events in Vilnius, Kaunas, Rumšiškės Open-Air Museum)
dates: Seventh week before Easter (Ash Wednesday equivalent in Lithuanian tradition); the festival begins on the night before Ash Wednesday
description: An ancient Lithuanian festival (IPA: [ʊʒˈɡaːvʲeːnʲeːs]) corresponding to Mardi Gras, Shrove Tuesday, and Carnaval in other Catholic traditions. The celebration begins on the night before Ash Wednesday when an effigy of winter (usually named Morė — also called Kotrė in Samogitia, or Gavėnas in Aukštaitija) is burned; ashes are scattered in the fields to drive away evil and welcome spring. The festival's central dramatic element is a staged battle between Lašininis ('porky', personifying winter) and Kanapinis ('hempen man', personifying spring), symbolizing the defeat of winter. Masquerade characters include: devils, witches, goats, the grim reaper, elderly bridegrooms, beggars, Gypsies, traveling Jewish merchants, witches, cranes, and roosters. Masked disguisers walk around villages, remaining unrecognizable to neighbours. Carnival performers parody everyday social types and supernatural entities. The traditional food of the holiday is pancakes (blynai) with a variety of toppings — eaten in large quantities as a farewell to fat/meat before Lent. The festival is pan-Lithuanian: major public events are held in Vilnius (Užupis and the old town), Kaunas, and the Rumšiškės Open-Air Ethnographic Museum, which hosts one of the most traditionally authentic celebrations.
tradition_type: Lithuanian Shrovetide / Winter Banishment Festival / Pre-Lenten Carnival
